entry.title: The Diplomat: 28 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Fearing US Sanctions, Philippines Scraps Russian Helicopter Deal
  • Sri Lanka’s State-Owned Enterprises Are a Big Part of Its Economic Problems
  • Japan’s Record-Breaking Summer COVID-19 Surge

  • India’s BJP Reaches Out to Nepal’s Political Parties

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

  • Print Edition 42

  • Overthrowing the Patriarchy Through Ecstatic Sex
  • What Oceanographers Can Learn From Their Animal Colleagues
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

ProPublica is an independent, nonprofit newsroom that produces investigative journalism with moral force.

entry.title: Nature: 28 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Monkeypox: wealthy countries must avoid their COVID-19 mistakes
  • Support Europe’s bold vision for responsible research assessment
  • Stop misusing data when hiring academics
  • Molecular motor, warm blood’s origin and researcher lay-offs
  • ‘We are bearing witness’: Health researchers navigate a post-Roe world
  • Hawaii law could break years-long astronomy impasse
  • A guide to Plan B: the UK’s vague strategy for post-Brexit science funding
  • CRISPR ‘cousin’ put to the test in landmark heart-disease trial
  • How to shrink AI’s ballooning carbon footprint
  • ‘Mathematics is an unknown land’: meet Fields Medal winner Maryna Viazovska
  • Exclusive: investigators found plagiarism and data falsification in work from prominent cancer lab
  • Make greenhouse-gas accounting reliable — build interoperable systems
  • Do two PhDs make twice the researcher?
  • Light-based sensors set to revolutionize on-site testing
  • The gravity of rockfalls
  • Evaporation of microwave-shielded polar molecules to quantum degeneracy
  • A device-independent quantum key distribution system for distant users
  • The sequences of 150,119 genomes in the UK Biobank
  • Hippocampal place cells have goal-oriented vector fields during navigation
  • Oocytes maintain ROS-free mitochondrial metabolism by suppressing complex I
  • ADAR1 mutation causes ZBP1-dependent immunopathology
  • ADAR1 averts fatal type I interferon induction by ZBP1
  • Mechanisms of APOBEC3 mutagenesis in human cancer cells
  • Author Correction: Bat coronaviruses related to SARS-CoV-2 and infectious for human cells
  • Author Correction: Catalogue of flat-band stoichiometric materials
  • Publisher Correction: Enhanced silica export in a future ocean triggers global diatom decline
  • Publisher Correction: Direct observation of the dead-cone effect in quantum chromodynamics
  • Publisher Correction: Nonlinear mechanics of human mitotic chromosomes
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

  • Do Good Doorbell Cams Make Good Neighbors?
  • 6 Apps That Take the Grind out of Your Workday
  • They Were ‘Calling to Help.’ Then They Stole Thousands
  • North Korea Hacked Him. So He Took Down Its Internet
  • Here Come the Underdogs of the Robot Olympics
  • Is It OK to Listen to a Butt-Dial Message?
  • This 22-Year-Old Builds Chips in His Parents’ Garage
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

  • Poem: 'On Visible Light'
  • Why Kids Are Afraid to Ask for Help
  • Science Needs to Shrink Its Carbon Footprint
Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: Harvard Business Review: 28 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: Harvard Business Review: 28 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-01 12:00PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • A Role That’s More Challenging Than Ever
  • A Seat at the Table Is Not Enough
  • A Common Parasite Can Make People More Entrepreneurial
  • Two Cofounders of Xendit on Pioneering Fintech in Southeast Asia
  • The C-Suite Skills That Matter Most
  • Is It Time to Consider Co-CEOs?
  • When Hiring CEOs, Focus on Character
  • As the World Shifts, So Should Leaders
  • Identifying Unmet Needs in a Digital Age
  • To Drive Diversity Efforts, Don’t Tiptoe Around Your Legal Risk
  • Private Equity Should Take the Lead in Sustainability
  • What You’re Getting Wrong About Customer Journeys
  • A Better Way to Put Your Data to Work
  • Leadership in a Politically Charged Age
  • Why You Need an AI Ethics Committee
  • How to Turn a Supply Chain Platform into an Innovation Engine
  • How to Overcome Your Fear of the Unknown
  • Case Study: Would Vegan Offerings Dilute Our Brand?
  • Exploring the Metaverse
  • Life’s Work: An Interview with Misty Copeland
Harvard Business Review is the leading destination for smart management thinking. Through its flagship magazine, books, and digital content and tools published on HBR.org, Harvard Business Review aims to provide professionals around the world with rigorous insights and best practices to help lead themselves and their organizations more effectively and to make a positive impact.

entry.title: Poetry: July/August 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Dear Reader,
  • we acknowledge ourselves
  • Hasísná
  • Emergence
  • hastiin k’aalógii ’ání
  • butterfly man tells a story
  • 1855
  • That you would be made of rock
  • sound installation
  • Did You Walk to Oklahoma?
  • Katuq.1
  • Examining the Phytolith
  • 𐓨𐓘͘ ́ 𐓺𐓟 𐓱𐓘́𐓱𐓘𐓺𐓟/Máze Htáhtaze/Typewriter
  • 𐓷𐓘𐓧𐓟́𐓺𐓟/Waléze/Stationery
  • Upon My Return to Appalachia
  • Cada día más cerca del fin del mundo
  • Shch’a-wa
  • Influx—Reservation Lockdown
  • Creation
  • Grocery Story
  • Echolocations
  • Red Stick Noonshine
  • Love Shack, Anadarko
  • Lost Jewels
  • Finding Home
  • in new countries more eloquent than elsewhere
  • nothing as beautiful as gravity in the eternal folds
  • 34°54’15’’ N 111°47’30’’ W
  • Shipwreck Pastoral
  • Hotbeds in Norfolk, Virginia
  • Said the Tobacco to the Hand
  • The Shore of This Day
  • Floaters
  • Papyrus Pantoum
  • sáanii kǫ’ yee da’ałt’eis tł’óo’gi
  • Women are cooking outside over the fire
  • Grandma’s Challenge
  • In the City of Eve
  • Cousin Wolf Sings
  • same word in chickasaw for wolf and coyote
  • From the Roof Deck
  • maghrib
  • midnight
  • Learning about Treaties in School
  • Soft Thunder
  • Emerging
  • The Meadow Views: Sword and Symbolic History
  • Bead Workers
  • Bird Killer
  • Northbound #23 Bus (Old Indian Trail #2)
  • Endangered Ghazal with Telegram and Natural World
  • A White Man in the Audience Said I Owed Him
  • Wavelength // Waveless
  • Interrogation in a Nail Salon
  • Of My Body
  • 杨柳枝词
  • The Song of the Willow Branches
  • Field Notes
  • before the dark
  • Well-Traveled Path: An Interview with Arthur Sze
Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.

entry.title: The Atlantic Magazine: July/August 2022
entry.summary: The Atlantic Magazine: July/August 2022 published at 2022-06-21 10:00AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • How Animals Perceive the World
  • Against Labels
  • American Rasputin
  • They Bent to Their Knees and Kissed the Sand
  • A Lost Trove of Civil War Gold, an FBI Excavation, and Some Very Angry Treasure Hunters
  • The Vindication of Jack White
  • We Have No Nuclear Strategy
  • My Daughter’s White Doll
  • The Book That Never Stops Changing
  • Beware the Luxury Beach Resort
  • White Author, Black Paragons
  • Why Is Dad So Mad?
  • Should We Blame Social Media for Society’s Divisions?
  • An Ode to My Thesaurus
  • Invitation
Current affairs and politics focussed on the US
